skill.name: migrate-to-shoehorn
skill.description: Guides migration of TypeScript test files from `as` assertions to @total-typescript/shoehorn helpers.
skill.summary: Replace unsafe TypeScript test assertions with Shoehorn helper calls.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: The static findings are mostly false positives caused by Markdown backticks, TypeScript `as` examples, and table text. Real command guidance is limited to package installation and local grep search. These are expected, but should remain user-approved.

low_findings.title: User-Initiated Package Installation and Search Commands
low_findings.description: The skill instructs users to install @total-typescript/shoehorn and search local test files. These commands are relevant to the migration task, but agents should request user approval before modifying dependencies or scanning repositories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly present and align with the documented workflow. The risk is low because there is no obfuscation, network exfiltration, dynamic execution, or prompt injection.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives
low_findings.description: Most reported shell backtick findings are Markdown inline code, TypeScript examples, or table entries. The weak cryptography and system reconnaissance flags do not match the observed context in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show prose, TypeScript assertion syntax, comments, or helper names rather than executable shell or cryptographic logic. No malicious instruction or hidden control flow was found in the reviewed file.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes explicit shell command examples for dependency installation and local grep-based discovery. These are legitimate migration steps, not autonomous execution paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are visible and specific. They are low risk because they are normal development commands and the skill contains no instruction to run them without user consent.

content.value_statement: TypeScript test fixtures often use broad `as` assertions that hide type problems. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through a focused migration to Shoehorn helpers.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to replace `as Type` with `fromPartial()` in test code.
Explains when to replace double assertions with `fromAny()` for negative tests.
Gives a grep pattern for finding test assertions in spec and test files.
Shows import patterns for Shoehorn helper functions.
limitations: It is intended for test code only, not production code.
It does not include an automated codemod or parser.
It assumes a TypeScript project with npm-compatible dependency management.
It does not verify every assertion is safe to migrate without human review.

use_cases.title: Clean Up Test Fixtures
use_cases.description: Replace broad assertions in large fixture objects with clearer Shoehorn helpers.

use_cases.title: Improve Type Safety in Tests
use_cases.description: Reduce unsafe `as` usage while keeping concise test data.

use_cases.title: Review Negative Test Inputs
use_cases.description: Choose `fromAny()` when tests intentionally pass invalid data.

prompt_templates.title: Find Migration Candidates
prompt_templates.prompt: Find TypeScript test files that use `as` assertions and summarize which ones are good candidates for Shoehorn.

prompt_templates.title: Migrate Simple Fixtures
prompt_templates.prompt: Migrate simple test fixtures from `as Type` assertions to `fromPartial()` and add the required Shoehorn import.

prompt_templates.title: Handle Invalid Test Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Review tests that use double assertions for intentionally invalid data and migrate appropriate cases to `fromAny()`.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Repository Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a phased migration plan for replacing test-only TypeScript assertions with Shoehorn across this repository.

output_examples.input: A test passes a partial request object with a direct type assertion.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends using `fromPartial()`, adding a Shoehorn import, and keeping the fixture focused on required fields.

output_examples.input: A negative test uses a double assertion to pass an invalid identifier.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends `fromAny()` because the invalid value is intentional and part of the error-path test.

output_examples.input: A team wants to remove unsafe assertions from all test files.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes installing Shoehorn, finding candidate files, migrating simple cases first, and running type checks after each batch.

best_practices: Use Shoehorn only in test files.
Run the TypeScript checker after each migration batch.
Review each assertion to choose `fromPartial()`, `fromAny()`, or `fromExact()` deliberately.
anti_patterns: Do not migrate production code to Shoehorn.
Do not replace every assertion without checking test intent.
Do not use `fromAny()` when the value should still type-check.

faq.question: What problem does this skill solve?
faq.answer: It helps replace unsafe TypeScript test assertions with Shoehorn helper functions.

faq.question: Is this for production code?
faq.answer: No. The skill explicitly says Shoehorn should be used only in test code.

faq.question: When should I use `fromPartial()`?
faq.answer: Use it when a test only needs part of a larger object and the supplied fields should type-check.

faq.question: When should I use `fromAny()`?
faq.answer: Use it when a test intentionally passes invalid data to exercise an error path.

faq.question: Does this skill automate the whole migration?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples, but a human or agent must apply and verify changes.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
